retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Transpulmonary Pressure -- Pathophysiology -- Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) and neonatal ARDS
retrieved_docs.content: ARDS is a lung injury due to acute inflammation that leads to increased vascular permeability. According to the Berlin definition, the onset of respiratory distress and hypoxia should be within one week of a known clinical insult or new or worsening symptoms. Chest imaging should reveal bilateral opacities, and pulmonary edema should not be from heart failure or due to fluid overload. [16] Determining the severity of ARDS is possible by calculating the PaO2/FiO2 ratio. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Prognosis of infants managed with antenatal steroids, respiratory support, and exogenous surfactant therapy is excellent. Mortality is less than 10%, with some studies showing survival rates of up to 98% with advanced care. Increased survival in developed countries is in stark comparison to babies who received no intervention in low-income countries, where the mortality rate for premature infants with RDS is significantly higher, at times close to 100%. [40] With adequate ventilatory support alone, surfactant production eventually begins, and once surfactant production begins along with the onset of diuresis, RDS improves within 4 or 5 days. Untreated disease leading to severe hypoxemia in the first days of life can result in multiple organ failure and death.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Review the etiology of neonatal respiratory distress syndrome. Outline the evaluation of neonatal respiratory distress syndrome. Summarize the management of respiratory distress syndrome in neonates. Describe the importance of interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and outcomes related to neonatal respiratory distress syndrome. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric and Neonatal Resuscitation -- Introduction -- Contexts of Pediatric Resuscitation Beyond the Perinatal Period
retrieved_docs.content: Unlike in adults, cardiac arrest after the perinatal period in children is usually due to respiratory failure or shock rather than primary cardiac disease. The mnemonic "Hs and Ts" summarizes the reversible causes: Hs: Hypovolemia, hypoxia, hydrogen ion imbalance, hypokalemia/hyperkalemia, hypothermia Ts: Tension pneumothorax, tamponade, toxins, thrombosis, trauma

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Transpulmonary Pressure -- Pathophysiology -- Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) and neonatal ARDS
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal ARDS is an acute pulmonary inflammatory disease resulting from the deficiency of pulmonary surfactant. Although there is no particular treatment, supportive measures such as ventilatory support, surfactant replacement, extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO), nutritional support, and liquid management are the cornerstones of management. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal respiratory distress syndrome, or RDS, is a common cause of respiratory distress in a newborn, presenting within hours after birth, most often immediately after delivery. RDS primarily affects preterm neonates, and infrequently, term infants. The incidence of RDS is inversely proportional to the gestational age of the infant, with more severe disease in the smaller and more premature neonates. While treatment modalities, including antenatal corticosteroids, surfactants, and advanced respiratory care of the neonate, have improved the outcomes for patients affected by RDS, it continues to be a leading cause of morbidity and mortality in the preterm infant.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal respiratory distress syndrome is a frequent cause of increased morbidity and mortality in neonates. Understanding the pathophysiology, clinical presentation, diagnosis, prevention, and management of this condition is vital to decreasing morbidity and mortality. This activity reviews the etiology, epidemiology, pathophysiology, evaluation, and management of respiratory distress syndrome in neonates, and discusses the role of the interprofessional team in evaluating and treating patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Transpulmonary Pressure -- Pathophysiology -- Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS) and neonatal ARDS
retrieved_docs.content: While the pathogenesis of these two conditions differs, the pathophysiological result and concepts are very similar. There is a loss of surfactant, which causes an imposing increase in alveolar surface tension, pressure, and resistance, which prevent lung expansion. As a result of an increase in alveolar pressure within the lung due to either fluid accumulation or inflammation, there becomes a net increase in transpulmonary pressure, which prevents airflow and lung expansion during inspiration.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Complications of neonatal respiratory distress syndrome are related mainly to the clinical course of RDS in neonates and the long term outcomes of the neonates. While surfactant therapy has decreased the morbidity associated with RDS, many patients continue to have complications during and after the acute course of RDS.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The goals of optimal management of neonatal respiratory distress syndrome include decreasing incidence and severity using antenatal corticosteroids, followed by optimal management using respiratory support, surfactant therapy, and overall care of the premature infant. Antenatal corticosteroids (discussed later in the topic) Monitoring oxygenation and ventilation Assisted ventilation of the neonate Exogenous surfactant therapy Supportive care, including thermoregulation, nutritional support, fluid and electrolyte management, antibiotic therapy, etc. [22] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Treatment / Management -- Assisted Ventilation of the Neonate
retrieved_docs.content: Mechanical Ventilation: Patients who do not respond to CPAP, develop respiratory acidosis (PH < 7.2 and PaCo2 > 60-65 mm of Hg), hypoxemia (PaO2 < 50 mm of Hg or Fio2 > 0.40 on CPAP), or severe apnea are managed with endotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation. The goals of mechanical ventilation include providing adequate respiratory support while balancing the risks of barotrauma, volutrauma, and oxygen toxicity. Time-cycled pressure limited ventilation is the preferred initial mode of ventilation in preterm infants with RDS. High-frequency oscillatory ventilation (HFOV) and high-frequency jet ventilation (HFJV) are often used as rescue modalities when requiring high conventional ventilator support or concerns for pulmonary air leaks. Other strategies include the use of high-frequency ventilation empirically in extremely preterm infants to minimize lung injury.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Treatment / Management -- Monitoring Oxygenation and Ventilation
retrieved_docs.content: Non-invasive pulse oximetry is now the standard of care to monitor oxygen saturation (SaO2). Unclear higher limits often limit the utility of pulse oximetry, since PaO2 could be significantly higher at the SaO2 levels >95%. Non-invasive capnography and transcutaneous carbon dioxide monitoring are used as adjuncts for monitoring ventilation.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Overdistension of the alveoli during positive pressure ventilation leads to further damage and inflammation. Besides, oxidative stress generated both by high oxygen tensions from mechanical ventilation and inflammatory processes within the lung also promotes the conversion of surfactant into an inactive form through protein oxidant damage and lipid peroxidation. [17] [18] [19] Thus RDS can cause hypoxemia through alveolar hyperventilation, diffusion abnormality, ventilation-perfusion mismatch, intrapulmonary shunting, or a combination of these mechanisms. This hypoxemia and tissue hypoperfusion ultimately lead to increased anaerobic metabolism at a cellular level with resultant lactic acidemia.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal respiratory distress syndrome (RDS) occurs from a deficiency of surfactant, due to either inadequate surfactant production, or surfactant inactivation in the context of immature lungs. Prematurity affects both these factors, thereby directly contributing to RDS.

retrieved_docs.title: Neonatal MIS-C -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: World Health Organization declared a COVID-19 pandemic in March 2020. SARS-CoV-2 causes COVID-19 infection. It presents as acute respiratory distress syndrome and infects all age groups. In adults, COVID-19 causes acute respiratory failure. In children, COVID-19 usually has a milder clinical course and more favorable outcome than in adults. In contrast to adults and older children, covid-19 infection is uncommon in neonates. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1707
retrieved_docs.content: Neonatal disorder (اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة) is a broad category of conditions affecting newborns. Respiratory distress syndrome is identified as a specific type of neonatal disorder, and the category also includes the child conditions fetal erythroblastosis (كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية) and premature rupture of membranes (رضح الولادة). Prevention and control measures are applicable to the overall neonatal disorder as well as to each of its constituent conditions—respiratory distress syndrome, fetal erythroblastosis, and premature rupture of membranes—underscoring the importance of proactive management in newborn care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1830
retrieved_docs.content: اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة (neonatal disorder) is a broad clinical category that includes specific conditions such as respiratory distress syndrome and كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية (excess fetal red erythroblasts). The inverse_isa relationship indicates that respiratory distress syndrome is a subtype of اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, while the has child relationship shows that كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية is a manifestation or sub‑condition within the same neonatal disorder. For each of these entities—اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, respiratory distress syndrome, and كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية—the qualifier “etiologie” is allowed, meaning that the cause or etiology can be specified for each condition. Recognizing and describing the etiological factors of the neonatal disorder and its constituent conditions is essential for accurate diagnosis and targeted management in newborn care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723
retrieved_docs.content: The term خديج designates infants who have completed 29–30 weeks of gestation, and this developmental stage is directly linked to the occurrence of متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). Consequently, babies classified as خديج are at risk for respiratory complications arising from lung immaturity, highlighting the importance of recognizing this gestational age category when assessing and managing neonatal respiratory health.

retrieved_docs.content: how extreme prematurity, short gestation, low birth weight, fetal growth restriction, respiratory distress, perinatal infection, birth trauma, and neonatal metabolic disorders are interlinked within a multilingual, multi‑coded framework that supports comprehensive patient care and data interoperability.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1552
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory distress syndrome is classified as a type of اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة (neonatal disorder). The broader category اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة includes the specific conditions كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية and رضح الولادة. Virologie is an allowed qualifier for اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, for respiratory distress syndrome, and also for كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية and رضح الولادة, indicating that viral factors may be relevant in the assessment and management of these neonatal disorders. This hierarchical organization links respiratory distress syndrome to other neonatal conditions and emphasizes the potential role of virology across the spectrum of neonatal pathology.

Clinically, it is included within **اضطرابات لدى حديث الولادة (SMQ)**, the neonatal disorders grouping, and is mapped from **حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة** (unspecified condition arising in the perinatal period). This perinatal condition is linked to **عدوى في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة، غير محدد بخلاف ذلك** (nonspecific perinatal infection) and has a broader relationship with **تقييد النمو الجنيني** (fetal growth restriction). **Respiratory distress syndrome** is cross‑referenced with **متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة** (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult) is a specific adult respiratory condition that is classified under the broader category of اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ (respiratory disorder).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 189
retrieved_docs.content: each condition as a potential adverse event, enabling systematic risk assessment, targeted monitoring, operative decision‑making, and coordinated maternal‑fetal and newborn care aimed at reducing morbidity and improving outcomes for both mother and infant.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1687
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory distress syndrome and respiratory failure ; insufficiency ; arrest (adult) are both specific forms of اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ (respiratory disorder). In addition, respiratory distress syndrome is also categorized as a type of اِضطِراب الرِّئَة (lung disorder). The broader category اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ itself is placed under الاِختِنَاق (asphyxia). Together, these hierarchical relationships show that respiratory distress syndrome belongs to both the general class of respiratory disorders and the more specific class of lung disorders, while respiratory failure ; insufficiency ; arrest (adult) is a subtype of respiratory disorder; and that all respiratory disorders are considered a subset of the overall condition of asphyxia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 223
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes a hierarchical framework that links gestational age, growth parameters, perinatal infections, and neonatal complications. Completion of 29‑30 weeks of gestation is classified as a preterm (خديج) condition and is considered a liveborn outcome; “liveborn” functions as a broad category that contains newborn disorders (اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة), which are a subclass of the preterm condition. Preterm birth is frequently associated with respiratory distress syndrome (متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة), also termed pulmonary surfactant deficiency syndrome, highlighting the causal link between early gestation and immature lung development. Respiratory distress syndrome is simultaneously classified as a respiratory disorder (اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ) and a lung disorder (اِضطِراب الرِّئَة), and is a member of the neonatal‑and‑newborn disorder set (اضطرابات لدى حديث الولادة, SMQ). In ICD‑10‑AM it maps to the code for اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ under the parent “Duration of gestation,” and within

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2792
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory distress syndrome is classified as a specific type of lung disorder (اِضطِراب الرِّئَة), indicating that it falls under the broader category of pulmonary disturbances. Recognizing this hierarchical relationship helps clinicians understand that respiratory distress syndrome shares fundamental characteristics with other lung disorders, which can guide accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment strategies, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1483
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory distress syndrome is identified across languages, with the Czech term syndrom dechové tísně and the Arabic term اِضطِراب الرِّئَة listed as its parent entries, and it is cross‑referenced to the Arabic designation متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة. These synonymous labels all describe the same clinical condition—a neonatal lung disorder caused by insufficient surfactant that leads to severe breathing difficulty—thereby supporting consistent recognition and management of the disease in multilingual medical contexts.
